perturbation.original_text: ‘Confidential Information’ means any information or sensitive data , which shall include but is not limited to, design, fabrication & assembly drawings, know-how, processes, product specifications, raw materials, trade secrets, market opportunities, or business or financial affairs of the Parties or their customers, product samples, inventions, concepts and Non-Disclosure Agreement 2any other technical and/or commercial information, disclosed directly or indirectly and in any form whatsoever (including, but not limited to, disclosure made in writing, oral or in the form of samples, models, computer programs, drawings or other instruments) furnished by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: ‘Confidential Information’ means any information or sensitive data, which shall include but is not limited to, design, fabrication & assembly drawings, know-how, processes, product specifications, raw materials, business opportunities, or business or financial affairs of the Parties or their customers, product samples, inventions, concepts and Non-Disclosure Agreement 2any other technical and/or commercial information, disclosed directly or indirectly and in any form whatsoever (including, but not limited to, disclosure made in writing, oral or in the form of samples, models, computer programs, drawings or other instruments) furnished by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party under this Agreement. Trade secrets are explicitly excluded from this definition.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' the contract conflicts with established legal protections for trade secrets. The definition of confidential information now conflicts with the legal definition of a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the UTSA, which protects trade secrets. By excluding trade secrets from the definition of Confidential Information, the contract fails to protect information that is legally defined and protected as a trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly excludes 'trade secrets' from the definition of 'Confidential Information'. This contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), as trade secrets are legally protected. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'trade secret' broadly, encompassing various forms of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, provided the owner has taken reasonable measures to keep it secret and the information derives independent economic value from not being generally known. By excluding trade secrets, the contract fails to provide the legally mandated protection for information that qualifies as a trade secret. This direct exclusion undermines the legal obligation to protect trade secrets, creating a clear contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a dead link and therefore doesn't impact this determination.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in force for five (5) years from the Effective Date, except to the extent this Agreement is superseded by stipulations of the Contemplated Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in force for one (1) year from the Effective Date, and will automatically renew annually unless explicitly terminated in writing by either party with a 90-day notice before renewal.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the contract term and adding an auto-renewal clause contradicts standard contract law principles regarding clear and unambiguous terms. The auto-renewal could be deemed unconscionable under certain circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts Section 25 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which addresses agreements without consideration and emphasizes the importance of free consent. The auto-renewal clause, if not clearly understood and agreed upon, can be challenged as lacking free consent or adequate consideration, particularly if it favors one party significantly. The one-year term directly conflicts with the original five-year term, making enforcement of either term uncertain.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: double jeopardy | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you double jeopardy The Double Jeopardy Clause of the Fifth Amendment of the U.S. Constitution protects against being prosecuted twice for the same crime . Through the incorporation doctrine , double jeopardy applies to both the federal and state governments, following Benton v. Maryland, 395 U.S. 784 (1969) . Double jeopardy generally covers criminal punishment, not all sanctions . In certain cases, civil penalties may qualify if they are punitive. In United States v. One Assortment of 89 Firearms, 465 U.S. 354 (1984) , the Supreme Court held that the prohibition on double jeopardy extends to civil sanctions which are applied in a manner that is punitive in nature. In United States v. Halper, 490 U.S. 435 (1989) , a civil sanction made under the False Claims Act qualifies as punishment if the sanction is overwhelmingly disproportionate in compensating the government for its loss, and if the disproportionate award can be explained only as a deterrent or as having a retributive purpose. In One Lot Emerald Cut Stones v. United States, 409 U.S. 232 (1972) , the Supreme Court held, "Congress may impose both a criminal and a civil sanction in respect to the same act or omission for the Double Jeopardy Clause prohibits merely punishing twice, or attempting a second time to punish criminally, for the same offense." However, in United States v. Ursery, 518 US 267 (1996) , the Supreme Court held that civil asset forfeitures did not constitute a "punishment" for purposes of the Double Jeopardy Clause because civil asset forfeiture is a remedial civil sanction, and not a punitive criminal sanction. See e.g., Currier v. Virginia, 138 S. Ct. 2144, 201 L. Ed. 2d 650 (2018) The protection also applies when someone is tried as a juvenile and then again as an adult for the same offense. In Breed v. Jones, 421 U.S. 519 (1975) , the Supreme Court decided that double jeopardy applies to an individual who is tried as a juvenile and is then later tried as an adult; this is because juvenile courts have the option to try a minor as an adult . If that court tries the individual as a juvenile, then another trial court may not try that individual as an adult for the same crime. For more on double jeopardy, see this Cornell Law Review article and this Yale School Legal Scholarship Repository article . [Last reviewed in July of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords constitutional amendment U.S. CONSTITUTION Fifth Amendment double jeopardy DOUBLE JEOPARDY CLAUSE Wex CIVICS the Constitution courts criminal law criminal procedure wex definitions constitutional law courts and procedure criminal law and procedure Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates a fixed term of five years. The changed text reduces this to one year with an auto-renewal clause. The Law Explanation argues this contradicts Section 25 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, concerning free consent and consideration, potentially making the auto-renewal unconscionable if not clearly understood. While the scraped snippets do not directly address this specific aspect of contract law, the altered term and addition of the auto-renewal clause introduce ambiguity and potential for unfairness, particularly if one party is significantly favored. The modification fundamentally changes the duration and renewal conditions of the agreement, creating a significant departure from the original terms and introducing elements that could be challenged based on principles of contract law related to clarity, consent, and consideration.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of India and in any dispute arising out of or relating to this agreement, the Parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts situated at Delhi, India.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of a jurisdiction of the Receiving Party's choosing, and any disputes will be settled through binding arbitration according to rules determined by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the specified jurisdiction (Delhi, India) and introduces a unilateral clause where the Receiving Party chooses the governing law and arbitration rules. This contradicts fundamental principles of contract law, which require mutual agreement on jurisdiction and dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 28(1)(a) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, mandates that arbitral tribunals decide disputes according to the rules designated by the parties. The modification allows one party to unilaterally determine the rules, contradicting this requirement and creating an unfair and potentially unenforceable arbitration agreement.
perturbation.justification: The modification shifts the power to determine the governing law and arbitration rules unilaterally to the Receiving Party, which is a contradiction. The original text specifies the jurisdiction of Delhi, India for dispute resolution, ensuring a mutually agreed-upon forum. The changed text undermines the principle of mutual agreement in contract law, as it allows one party to dictate the terms of dispute resolution. The Law Explanation cites Section 28(1)(a) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, which reinforces the need for arbitral tribunals to decide disputes according to rules designated by the parties, which is further evidence that a unilateral choice contradicts the principle of mutual agreement.
